title: [PAPER] [2603.09901] Has quantum advantage been achieved?
why:
- Despite initial excitement after Google's 2019 quantum supremacy experiment, less than half of surveyed quantum computing experts now believe quantum advantage has been achieved, revealing skepticism in the field.
- The main criticism of previous "quantum advantage" experiments centers on the fact that their results were soon simulated by classical computers, undermining claims of advantage.
- Recent demonstrations of quantum advantage focus on random circuit sampling (RCS), a task considered classically hard, and involve programmable quantum devices executing specific input circuits.
- Formal criteria for quantum advantage include programmability, a scaling (ideally exponential) time gap between quantum and classical solutions, and a task not solvable by any classical computer at the time.
- Experimental milestones include Google's first quantum supremacy experiment with 53 superconducting qubits and similar follow-ups by other teams, progressively improving in size and fidelity.
